disease.name: HELLP Syndrome
disease.note: Life-threatening pregnancy complication characterized by Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, and Low Platelets. Variant of severe preeclampsia. Usually occurs in third trimester or postpartum. High maternal and perinatal mortality if unrecognized. Prompt delivery is definitive treatment. Can present without hypertension or proteinuria (20% of cases).

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Mississippi Class 1 (platelets <50K, AST/ALT >70, LDH >600) -> DELIVER within 24h; Class 2 (platelets 50-100K) -> DELIVER within 48h; Class 3 (platelets 100-150K) -> DELIVER after steroids if <34w

presentingComplaints: Right upper abdominal or epigastric pain

historyFeatures.note: Classic presenting symptom; often severe and constant
Often mild or late; may be absent

bedside.name: Blood pressure measurement (automated or manual)
bedside.indication: hypertension (systolic >=140 or diastolic >=90) in most cases

bedside.name: Urine dipstick for protein
bedside.indication: proteinuria (1+ or more) in 80% of HELLP; may be absent

bedside.name: Patellar reflex and clonus assessment
bedside.indication: hyperreflexia from CNS irritability; clonus indicates severe preeclampsia

laboratory.name: FBC with platelet count
laboratory.indication: thrombocytopenia: platelets <100,000 defines HELLP; severity grading per Mississippi classification

laboratory.name: Peripheral blood smear
laboratory.indication: microangiopathic hemolytic anemia: schistocytes, helmet cells, spherocytes; hallmark finding

laboratory.name: LDH
laboratory.indication: elevated >600 U/L due to hemolysis; correlates with disease severity

laboratory.name: AST and ALT
laboratory.indication: elevated >70 IU/L; indicates hepatic injury; AST > ALT typically

laboratory.name: Total and direct bilirubin
laboratory.indication: elevated bilirubin (unconjugated from hemolysis, also conjugated from liver dysfunction)

laboratory.name: Serum creatinine
laboratory.indication: elevated in acute kidney injury; monitor for deterioration

laboratory.name: Uric acid
laboratory.indication: elevated in preeclampsia; helps distinguish from AFLP

laboratory.name: INR/PT/PTT
laboratory.indication: coagulation profile; prolonged if DIC develops; platelet count <50K may have prolonged bleeding time

laboratory.name: Glucose
laboratory.indication: hypoglycemia more suggestive of acute fatty liver of pregnancy

laboratory.name: 24-hour urine protein (if feasible)
laboratory.indication: quantify proteinuria; >300mg in 24h supports preeclampsia diagnosis

imaging.name: Obstetric ultrasound with fetal assessment
imaging.indication: fetal well-being, growth, amniotic fluid, placental appearance; rule out abruption

imaging.name: Abdominal ultrasound
imaging.indication: liver: subcapsular hematoma, hepatic rupture; rule out gallstones

imaging.name: CT abdomen (if hepatic rupture suspected)
imaging.indication: confirm hepatic hematoma or rupture; urgent surgical planning

management.protocolReference: ACOG Practice Bulletin: HELLP Syndrome; NICE Guideline: Hypertensive Disorders in Pregnancy; WHO Recommendations: Preeclampsia and Eclampsia

pathways.criteria: Platelets 100,000-150,000; AST/ALT elevated; LDH >600; no severe features; gestational age <34 weeks
pathways.treatment: Admit to high-dependency unit. Complete course of antenatal corticosteroids (betamethasone 12mg IM q24h x 2 doses) if <34 weeks. Magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis: 4g IV loading over 20 min + 1g/h IV infusion x 24h. Antihypertensives if BP >160/105: labetalol 200mg PO, or nifedipine MR 10-20mg PO, or hydralazine 5-10mg IV. Monitor platelets, AST, LDH, Cr q6h. If improving after steroids and <34w, consider expectant management (very cautiously). Deliver by 34 weeks.

pathways.criteria: Platelets 50,000-100,000; AST >70; LDH >600; gestational age >34 weeks or <34w after steroid course
pathways.treatment: Admit to ICU/HDU. Deliver within 48h after steroid course if <34w; if >34w or fetal distress, deliver within 24h. Magnesium sulfate IV as above for seizure prophylaxis (continue for 24h postpartum). Antihypertensives as needed. Corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity if <34w. Mode of delivery: vaginal if favorable cervical status and no fetal distress; cesarean for usual obstetric indications. Careful anesthetic planning: avoid regional anesthesia if platelets <70,000.

pathways.criteria: Platelets <50,000; AST >70; LDH >600; any severe feature (hepatic hematoma, DIC, renal failure, pulmonary edema, eclampsia)
pathways.treatment: URGENT ICU admission. Stabilize before delivery: magnesium sulfate IV, antihypertensives for BP control. Deliver within 24h regardless of gestational age (may need to deliver within hours if maternal instability). Correct coagulopathy if needed: platelet transfusion if platelets <20,000 for cesarean or <50,000 for surgery; FFP if DIC. Cesarean is usually required due to maternal instability. Avoid general anesthesia if possible (airway edema, hypertensive response). If hepatic hematoma: conservative if stable; embolization or surgical repair if expanding or ruptured. Postpartum: continue magnesium x 24h; monitor platelets, LFTs, BP; HELLP may transiently worsen postpartum before improving.

pathways.criteria: HELLP syndrome diagnosed after delivery (25-30% of HELLP presents postpartum; may worsen before improving)
pathways.treatment: Same principles as antepartum. Magnesium sulfate for 24h postpartum. Antihypertensives. Monitor labs until improving. Platelet count typically nadirs at 24-48h postpartum. If not improving within 72h, reconsider alternative diagnoses (TTP, aHUS, SLE flare). Most recover fully within 48-72h. Counsel about recurrence risk (5-10%) and need for low-dose aspirin 75-150mg daily in future pregnancies from 12 weeks.
